translatedText: Research on Unified Field Theory
Made several attempts to construct a unified field theory that unifies gravity, electromagnetism, and nuclear forces.
Research on Color Theory
Showed strong interest in psychology, especially color perception and colorimetry, and published a series of papers in this field.
For what achievement did Erwin Schrödinger receive the Nobel Prize?
He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1933, jointly with Paul Dirac, for the formulation of the Schrödinger equation.
What is Schrödinger's cat?
It is a famous thought experiment devised to mock the Copenhagen interpretation of quantum mechanics.
What book did Schrödinger write?
He wrote 'What Is Life?', which includes discussions on the genetic code of living organisms and negentropy.
When and where was Schrödinger born, and when did he die?
He was born on August 12, 1887, in Vienna, Austria, and died of tuberculosis in Vienna on January 4, 1961, at the age of 73.
Why did Schrödinger leave Germany?
He left Germany in 1933 because he disliked the Nazi's anti-Semitism.
July 4, 1807
Giuseppe Maria Garibaldi, Italian general and patriot, was born.
Certified as a merchant marine captain.
April 1833
Met Giovanni Battista Cuneo, an Italian political immigrant, in Taganrog, Russia, and joined the Young Italy movement.
February 1834
Sentenced to death after participating in the Piedmont uprising and fled to Marseille.
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil and helped the independence of the Riograndense Republic.
October 1839
Anita Ribeiro joined Garibaldi's ship, the 'Rio Pardo'.
First son, Menotti, was born.
Moved to Montevideo and worked as a trader and teacher.
Married Anita in Montevideo; took command of the Uruguayan fleet and raised the 'Italian Legion'.
Second daughter, Rosita, was born; formed the Italian Legion in Montevideo.
Third daughter, Teresita, was born.
Won the battles of Cerro and San Antonio.
January 1848
Returned to his home country, Italy, upon hearing news of the outbreak of revolution in Palermo.
Promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army; fled to northern Italy after the fall of the Roman Republic; his wife Anita died during the retreat.
Became a resident of New York.
March 21, 1854
Sailed to the mouth of the River Tyne in North East England as master of the sailing ship Commonwealth.
Returned to Italy.
April 1859
Outbreak of the Second Italian War of Independence; led the 'Hunters of the Alps' and captured Varese and Como.
Landed at Marsala, Sicily, with a volunteer army of a thousand men.
Defeated the Bourbon French garrison at Calatafimi.
Launched the siege of Palermo.
September 7, 1860
Entered the capital of Naples.
October 1, 1860
Volunteers achieved success at the Battle of the Volturno.
October 26, 1860
Met and shook hands with Victor Emmanuel II in Teano.
March 17, 1861
The unified Kingdom of Italy was established.
June 1862
Left Genoa and landed in Palermo to gather volunteers under the slogan 'Rome or Death'.
August 14, 1862
Landed at Melito and marched to the Calabrian mountains.
August 28, 1862
Met regular troops at Aspromonte and was taken prisoner after a battle.
With the backing of the Italian government, recruited the 'Hunters of the Alps', led them to Trentino, and defeated the Austrian forces at Bezzecca.
Marched on Rome again but was stopped by the Papal army supported by French reinforcements.
July 1870
Franco-Prussian War broke out.
June 2, 1882
Died on the island of Caprera.
Contribution to the Unification of Italy
Contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.
Hero of the Two Worlds
Known as the 'Hero of the Two Worlds' for his military achievements across South America and Europe.
Support for South American Independence
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.
Italian Military Command
Was appointed general by the provisional government of Milan and promoted to Marshal of the Roman Republic army.
Command of the Hunters of the Alps
Led the 'Hunters of the Alps' in the Second Italian War of Independence and captured Varese and Como.
Success of the Expedition of the Thousand
Led an expedition of a thousand men, successfully annexing Sicily, Southern Italy, Marche, and Umbria to the Kingdom of Sardinia.
Participation in the Franco-Prussian War
Served as commander of the Army of the Vosges during the Franco-Prussian War.
International Symbol
Became an international symbol of realizing the ideals of national independence and republicanism.
Efforts to Limit Papal Power
Contributed to anti-clericalism among Latin liberals and did much to limit the temporal power of the Pope.
Who is Giuseppe Garibaldi?
An Italian general and patriot who contributed to the unification of Italy and the establishment of the Kingdom of Italy.
Why is Garibaldi called the 'Hero of the Two Worlds'?
Because his military achievements span across South America and Europe.
What were Garibaldi's political beliefs?
He was a republican dedicated to unifying Italy under a democratic republican government.
What is Garibaldi's iconic attire?
It is associated with the red shirts worn by his volunteers in lieu of a uniform.
What activities did Garibaldi undertake in South America?
Joined the Ragamuffin rebels in Brazil to help the independence of the Riograndense Republic, and also participated in the Uruguayan Civil War, raising the Redshirts.
Did Garibaldi have the opportunity to participate in the American Civil War?

He was offered a commission as a major general in the Union Army by President Abraham Lincoln, but the offer fell through because he demanded the abolition of slavery and the position of commander-in-chief of the army.
Where is Garibaldi's hometown?
He was born in Nice (now in France), which was under French rule in 1792.
Who did Garibaldi conflict with after the unification of Italy?
He deeply disliked the Piedmontese Prime Minister Camillo di Cavour, due to Cavour's pragmatism and a personal grudge over the cession of Nice to the French.
Born in Edinburgh, Scotland
Mother died (at age 8)
Produced his first scientific work, 'Oval Curves', at age 14
Entered the University of Edinburgh
Began studies at the University of Cambridge
Graduated with a degree in mathematics from Trinity College, Cambridge, placing second in the Tripos exam and first in the Smith's Prize exam
Elected a fellow of Trinity College, conducting experiments on electricity and magnetism and researching color theory
Published a paper on the structure of Saturn's rings
Married Katherine Mary Dewar
Won the Adams Prize for his research on Saturn's rings
Appointed Professor of Natural Philosophy and Astronomy at King's College London, and won the Rumford Medal from the Royal Society for his work on color vision
Produced the first color photograph by applying the mixture of the three primary colors
Published 'A Dynamical Theory of the Electromagnetic Field'
Resigned his professorship at King's College and became a professor at Cambridge University
Formulated the kinetic theory of gases statistically
Researched at the Cavendish Laboratory
Published 'A Treatise on Electricity and Magnetism'
Began editing the unpublished electrical experiments of Henry Cavendish
Died of stomach cancer in Cambridge on November 5 at the age of 48
